artifacts.content:
Subject: Juggling spring schedules (and a few other curveballs)

Hey Em,

I’m at the kitchen table at 44 Seabreeze, kids finally down, looking at February–June and wondering how we’re going to keep all these plates spinning.

Big one is Sarah. Her chemo at the QEII Health Sciences Centre is booked every second Thursday afternoon from March 6 through June 26, 2026. Her oncologist told her her immunity will be really low for 48–72 hours after each treatment, so I can’t have her doing on‑deck COVID screening or contact‑tracing those nights. I’m going to build a staffing grid and plug in backups for those Thursdays and the following weekends.

On top of that, I still need to keep the first Monday of each month clear of early‑morning deck time for my 7:30 a.m. CBT with Dr. Helen Fraser at the Nova Scotia Hospital Community Mental Health clinic in Dartmouth. You know what I’m like after those sessions since the whole March 2020 shutdown stuff – I’m wiped, and it’s not safe to jump straight into a 6:00–9:00 a.m. Performance block.

We also have to think about Sophie. After her January 10, 2026 review at the IWK Health Centre, her asthma specialist said no more than 60 minutes in any indoor block when the pool deck humidity is high. That means if she’s at Harbour City Community Aquatic Centre during Senior Dev, I may need to step off deck in the last 15 minutes to walk her out if she’s hitting the inhaler or starts coughing.

Can we sit down Sunday night, after the kids are in bed, and map out who’s doing Dalplex / Centennial / Canada Games Centre runs, and where your clinic shifts at Dal and the QEII fit? I don’t want to promise the club more than we can actually manage as a family.

Love,
Dan

metadata.subject: Juggling spring schedules (and a few other curveballs)

artifacts.content:
Title: Spring 2026 coaching + family constraints – working draft

1. Sarah’s availability
- Chemo at QEII Health Sciences Centre every second Thursday afternoon, March 6–June 26, 2026.
- Her oncologist warned her immunity will be especially low for 48–72 hours post‑treatment.
- Action: schedule backup COVID screener/contact tracer for those Thursdays + Fri/Sat sessions. Avoid putting her on deck or in close contact roles those windows.

2. My own capacity
- Standing CBT with Dr. Helen Fraser at Nova Scotia Hospital Community Mental Health (Dartmouth) – 7:30–8:30 a.m., first Monday of each month.
- Started after burnout/anxiety from March 2020 shutdown; I’m usually mentally drained afterward.
- Action: no 6:00–9:00 a.m. deck duties those Mondays. Shift Performance to assistant coach or move to later slot.

3. Sophie’s asthma limits
- IWK Health Centre review Jan 10, 2026: max 60 minutes in indoor pool sessions when deck humidity is high.
- Watch for increased inhaler use or coughing in last 15 minutes.
- Action: when she’s at Harbour City Community Aquatic Centre or Centennial during Senior Dev, pre‑plan an exit at 45–60 minute mark. Ensure another coach can cover if I need to walk her out.

4. Liam’s anxiety/night terrors
- Pending IWK child psych consult; Maggie wants to talk through whether he should delay Junior group.
- Action: Feb 11, 2026 call with Mum (8:15–9:00 p.m.) from home office at Seabreeze.

5. Reminder: build all of this into return‑to‑pool phase‑in starting Feb 15 so I’m not scrambling week‑of.

metadata.title: Spring 2026 coaching + family constraints – working draft

artifacts.content:
Subject: Why I’m obsessed with ending on time at CGC

Hey Colin,

Figured I’d explain why I’ve turned into the time police on Tuesday/Thursday nights at the Canada Games Centre.

We’re still catching up on that $4,200 tax payment plan with CRA from when my hours were cut April–June 2020. Then CGC nailed us with two late pool exit fees last fall – $150 each on October 3 and November 7, 2025 – because I let Performance and Senior Dev run 10–15 minutes over. That extra $300, on top of the CRA payments, really punched a hole in the household budget.

So now I’m building in a hard 10‑minute buffer at the end of each block. When I say “last repeat,” I mean it. No extra 50s, no “one more start.” I’d rather leave them wanting more than get another invoice from the facility.

Complicating Sundays too: I’m trying to keep my Centennial Pool blocks flexible because of your custody mess. With the ongoing mediation at Halifax Family Court and you being the “on paper” parent for those Sunday visits, I’m still the designated emergency contact if things go sideways at your ex’s apartment on Gottingen Street. Being pulled off deck twice since September 2025 to grab my nephew was a wake‑up call – I need an assistant coach on site any Sunday evening I’m primary.

Anyway, that’s the story. It’s not that I’ve suddenly become a hard‑nosed drill sergeant; I’m just tired of seeing coaching decisions show up as line items on our bank statement.

Let’s grab a coffee near Hollis Street after one of my visits with Mum and Dad and talk through your next court date.

Dan

metadata.subject: Why I’m obsessed with ending on time at CGC
metadata.location: Harbour City Community Aquatic Centre office

artifacts.content:
Title: Detailed oncology‑families email – return to pool

Date/Time: January 28, 2026, 8:00–9:00 p.m.
Location: Home office – 44 Seabreeze Crescent
Attendees: Dan (solo drafting)

Notes:

– Block this hour to write and send the detailed update from my Harbour City Aquatics address to the eight parents whose kids are immunocompromised.
– CC Sophie’s pediatric oncologist at the IWK Health Centre, Dr. Patel, who specifically asked to be copied.
– Explain clearly how the Phase 3 return‑to‑swimming plan reduces infection risk for post‑chemotherapy swimmers:
  • lane 1 (shallow end) at Centennial Pool reserved for them for first 4–6 weeks,
  • closest lane to emergency exit for quick removal if they feel unwell,
  • extra spacing on deck and stricter mask expectations for parents.
– Include details on cleaning protocols between cohorts and how Kinduct symptom flags will be handled.
– Reassure them that we can adjust start/finish times if fatigue is an issue.

To‑do before event:
– By Jan 25: finalize bullet points and run the medical phrasing by Emily and, if possible, Dr. Patel via quick email.
– Pull in notes from the general Return to Swimming Plan email and the Safety Snapshot templates so messaging is consistent.
– Remember to mention Sophie’s own situation so families know I’m making these accommodations as a parent as well as head coach.

Follow‑up:
– Save email as PDF, upload to secure club docs (restricted access).
– Add reminder to revisit arrangements after the first 4–6 weeks based on feedback from parents and Dr. Patel.

metadata.title: Detailed oncology‑families email – return to pool

artifacts.content:
Title: Evening comms routine – WhatsApp + sensitive families

Working checklist for Phase 3 (Feb–March 2026):

9:30 p.m. nightly reminder (phone alarm):
– Send WhatsApp updates from my personal mobile (902‑555‑2394) to each squad group with next‑day arrival times, mask reminders, and “complete Kinduct by X p.m.”
– For transparency, occasionally attach a screenshot from the daily symptom‑tracking Google Sheet (names, recent fevers, self‑isolation notes after travel) so parents see who’s already flagged and staying home.

Individual follow‑ups:
– Sarah Thompson: quick check‑ins about her son’s asthma action plan and whether he’s cleared for higher‑intensity sets, especially on nights close to her chemo blocks at QEII.
– Colin: coordinate picking up Sophie and Liam from the Dalplex pool on evenings Emily is on shift at the QEII Health Sciences Centre so I can stay on deck.
– Ahmed’s family (Syrian newcomers, North End): every Sunday at 7:30 p.m., call with our volunteer translator to walk them through the week’s safety precautions in Arabic – Kinduct steps, masking, and what “stay home” actually looks like when someone in the house has symptoms.

Other special comms:
– Senior swimmer with March 2023 custody order: always send duplicate, detailed safety‑precaution emails to both parents plus the mother’s lawyer at McInnes Cooper so there’s a clear written record of our mask, screening, and attendance rules.
– Medically vulnerable kids (e.g., 9‑year‑old in Sophie’s lane with IWK‑confirmed asthma/EpiPen Feb 2022): maintain separate, more detailed emails about stricter mask expectations, when Kinduct flags mean staying home, and our 911/IWK escalation plan.

Goal: make sure no one misses crucial info because of language, legal, or health complications.

metadata.title: Evening comms routine – WhatsApp + sensitive families

artifacts.content:
Subject: Fee‑relief families + the skeptics call

Hey Em,

Quick download while you’re between patients.

I’ve blocked off February 5, 2026 after evening practice to send a more candid email from my Harbour City Aquatics address to the 12 families I put on informal payment plans during COVID. I’m going to explain how the new return‑to‑swimming schedule affects their reduced‑fee arrangements and gently remind them that any outstanding balances over $600 need to be brought under $200 by the end of March if their kids are going to stay on the Performance or Senior Development rosters. Not my favourite part of the job, but we can’t keep running a deficit.

Before that, on February 3 at 7:30 p.m., I’ve scheduled a separate Zoom with the three most vocal COVID‑protocol skeptics – including Dad (Robert, from his masters lane at Centennial Pool) and Mark O’Rourke. They keep grumbling about mask rules and vaccine disclosure. I’m going to walk them through the plan again and record the call so there’s a clear record that we covered distancing, screening, and why we’re sticking with Kinduct.

On the home front, Mum and Dad are still on my mind. With Dad’s Type 2 diabetes diagnosis from the QEII back in October 2019 and them both in their late 60s at the Hollis Street condo, I told all the swim parents in last Sunday’s Safety Snapshot that any time I visit them I’ll stay away from the pool for 72 hours afterward and email the families so they can decide if they’re comfortable sending their kids that week.

Remind me tonight to ask you if that sounds overly cautious or just right.

Love,
Dan

Subject: Quick update on how we’re handling COVID notifications

Hi Sarah,

Just wanted to put in writing what we’ve been doing (and what I’d like to keep doing) around positive cases in the club as we ramp up for the Feb 15 return.

Given what happened back in late May 2020, when Sophie’s classmate’s mum tested positive and the news was all over the school WhatsApp before we even had guidance, I think our current low‑key system is the right balance. Parents who get a positive result email you directly at your personal address and copy me, rather than blasting the squad group chats. Then you and I quietly track any new positives, check the Kinduct logs, and email all close‑contact families within 12 hours with updated safety precautions and isolation expectations.

For the more complicated situations – like the senior swimmer whose parents have that March 2023 family court order – I’ve started sending duplicate, detailed safety‑rules emails to both parents’ personal addresses and cc’ing the mother’s lawyer at McInnes Cooper. That way there’s a clear written record of our mask, screening, and attendance policies and we’re not dragged into “he said, she said” later.

On the newcomer side, I’ll keep doing the Sunday 7:30 p.m. phone calls with Ahmed’s family in the North End, using our volunteer translator, to walk through the week’s precautions in Arabic. With his restaurant job gone since April 2020 and them on income support, they don’t always have data for Zoom or to download PDFs, so those calls are the only way to be sure they understand the Kinduct checks and mask rules.

If you see any holes in this, let’s tweak before February.

Dan
